newsletters.title: EGFR Lung Cancer Therapy Update
newsletters.keyTakeaway: New trial data may change monitoring and sequencing conversations for EGFR-positive metastatic lung cancer.
newsletters.content:
Clinical context: This issue focuses on EGFR-positive metastatic non-small cell lung cancer and how recent phase III data may affect monitoring and treatment-sequencing conversations. The lead article reviews outcomes for patients with exon 19 deletion and L858R mutations, with particular attention to intracranial disease control, time to CNS progression, and the practical question of when to reassess molecular status after progression on first-line osimertinib.

Key data discussed: The newsletter summarizes a new dataset showing improved intracranial response durability in a subgroup with baseline CNS risk, plus a separate expert commentary on sequencing after osimertinib. It highlights that clinicians may need to review MRI surveillance cadence, document CNS symptom triggers more explicitly, and plan earlier molecular re-testing when systemic progression is suspected.

Practice implications: For thoracic oncology teams, the most relevant section is the decision support checklist: confirm EGFR mutation subtype, assess CNS risk, document current TKI exposure, decide whether plasma or tissue re-testing is feasible, and discuss trial referral for resistance mechanisms when available. The article is framed as information triage and does not recommend an individual treatment choice.

Safety and operations notes: A short safety sidebar reminds teams to review pneumonitis risk, dermatologic toxicity, diarrhea management, and drug interaction checks when treatment changes are being considered. The final section includes administrative tumor board dates and a registration reminder, which are lower relevance than the clinical update.

newsletters.title: Heart Failure Guideline Brief
clinicalSignals: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
SGLT2 inhibitor use in heart failure
newsletters.keyTakeaway: Updated guidance emphasizes early optimization of foundational therapy and iron deficiency review in HFrEF.
newsletters.content:
Clinical context: This brief covers heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and the persistent gap between guideline recommendations and real-world therapy optimization. It opens with a short case vignette of a patient recently discharged after decompensation, then maps the follow-up visit to medication review, volume status, renal function, and patient education.

Key guidance points: The newsletter emphasizes earlier review of foundational therapy, including ARNI or ACE/ARB status, beta blocker titration, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist eligibility, and SGLT2 inhibitor continuation. It also flags iron deficiency screening as a recurring missed opportunity, especially for patients with ongoing fatigue, recent hospitalization, or reduced exercise tolerance.

Practice implications: The most actionable section is a visit checklist for HFrEF review: confirm current doses, check blood pressure and renal function, assess congestion, review SGLT2 inhibitor tolerance, screen for iron deficiency, and schedule follow-up after recent medication changes. A short sidebar suggests how practices can standardize home weight monitoring education.

Safety and operations notes: The brief includes cautions around renal dosing, hypotension, hyperkalemia, and post-discharge timing. It also includes a downloadable patient handout and a webinar registration link, but those are secondary to the clinical workflow update.

newsletters.title: Diabetes and CKD Medication Update
newsletters.keyTakeaway: The update summarizes kidney-risk reduction data for diabetes patients with albuminuria and obesity-related metabolic risk.
newsletters.content:
Clinical context: This update is written for clinicians managing type 2 diabetes with chronic kidney disease, albuminuria, obesity, and cardiovascular risk. It starts with the observation that many patients with diabetes and CKD have separate medication reviews for glycemic control, kidney protection, and weight management, even though these decisions often overlap.

Key evidence discussed: The newsletter summarizes kidney-risk reduction data for SGLT2 inhibitors, including albuminuria-related endpoints and practical continuation thresholds when kidney function declines. It also reviews GLP-1 receptor agonist considerations for patients with obesity-related metabolic risk and discusses how weight, adherence, gastrointestinal tolerability, and cardiovascular risk may influence shared decision-making.

Practice implications: The recommended follow-up checklist asks clinicians to review eGFR trend, UACR category, current SGLT2 inhibitor use, GLP-1 receptor agonist suitability, hypoglycemia risk, blood pressure targets, statin use, and patient goals for weight and kidney-risk reduction. The newsletter includes a short section on patient counseling language for explaining why kidney-protective therapy may be continued even when glucose effects are modest.

Safety and operations notes: The safety sidebar covers sick-day rules, genital infection counseling, dehydration risk, gastrointestinal tolerability, and coordination with nephrology when albuminuria progresses. The final section lists upcoming metabolic clinic education events.

newsletters.title: Rheumatology Biologic Safety Alert
newsletters.keyTakeaway: Safety checklist updates may affect targeted therapy reviews in inflammatory arthritis.
newsletters.content:
Clinical context: This safety alert is aimed at rheumatology teams caring for patients with inflammatory arthritis who receive targeted synthetic or biologic therapy. It focuses on rheumatoid arthritis patients on JAK inhibitors and psoriatic arthritis patients receiving IL-17 inhibitors, with a practical lens on infection-risk review before therapy continuation or switching.

Key safety updates: The newsletter reviews herpes zoster vaccination timing, tuberculosis screening documentation, recurrent infection history, and coordination with primary care for age-appropriate vaccination. It highlights how missed vaccination status can delay therapy changes and how incomplete infection-risk documentation can complicate shared decision-making.

Practice implications: The checklist section recommends confirming current targeted therapy, last infection episode, vaccination status, TB screening date, steroid exposure, biologic sequencing history, and patient-reported infection concerns. It includes a short example of how to document risk review before switching therapy in rheumatoid arthritis or psoriatic arthritis.

Safety and operations notes: The newsletter does not recommend a specific drug choice. It frames the information as a clinic workflow and safety documentation update. A final operations section lists an upcoming inflammatory disease webinar and template language for nurse-led pre-visit screening.

newsletters.title: Oncology Supportive Care Brief
newsletters.keyTakeaway: Supportive-care updates may help teams triage immune-related toxicity and prevent avoidable treatment interruptions.
newsletters.content:
Clinical context: This supportive-care brief is written for oncology nurses, clinical pharmacists, and day-unit teams who manage adverse-event calls, treatment education, antiemetic planning, and medication safety. It focuses on avoiding preventable treatment interruptions by improving triage of immune-related toxicity, chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting, febrile neutropenia risk, and oral targeted therapy adherence.

Key workflow updates: The newsletter summarizes a revised call-triage script for suspected immune-related adverse events, including symptom onset, grade indicators, steroid exposure, and when to escalate to the treating oncologist. It also reviews antiemetic planning for highly emetogenic regimens, with attention to breakthrough nausea, delayed symptoms, and patient-specific barriers to adherence.

Practice implications: The most actionable section is a supportive-care huddle checklist: confirm regimen emetogenicity, document antiemetic plan, flag neutropenia risk, review fever instructions, check oral therapy interactions, and ensure patients know which symptoms require urgent contact. It includes sample counseling points for oral TKI adherence and a reminder to document medication reconciliation before each cycle.

Safety and operations notes: The newsletter includes operational suggestions for nurse-pharmacist collaboration and a short case example where early immune-related adverse-event triage prevented an avoidable emergency visit. It also includes a low-priority note about a staff education session.

newsletters.title: Annual Congress Registration and Parking Update
newsletters.keyTakeaway: Administrative logistics for an upcoming congress and badge pickup.
newsletters.content:
Administrative context: Registration is open for the annual hospital education congress. The newsletter lists badge pickup windows, parking instructions, room changes, catering details, and reminders about session sign-in requirements.

Operational details: Attendees are asked to collect badges between 07:30 and 09:00, use the east visitor parking garage, and check the updated room map before arriving. The email includes a list of plenary session titles, but it does not provide disease-specific, medication-specific, patient-panel-specific, or practice-changing clinical content.

Relevance implications: This item may be useful for general logistics but should not trigger a clinical push based on patient data. It is included in the demo to show that the engine can suppress administrative newsletters even when they are legitimate communications.
